title: Approach to Articular and Musculoskeletal Disorders
section: Disorders of the Joints and Adjacent Tissues

items.subtopic: Approach to Articular and Musculoskeletal Disorders
items.title: Identifying Red Flag Conditions in Musculoskeletal Complaints
items.content: In musculoskeletal evaluations, it is crucial to identify red flag conditions such as septic arthritis, acute crystal-induced arthritis (e.g., gout), and fractures. These conditions are characterized by an acute onset of monoarticular or focal musculoskeletal pain and require prompt diagnosis to prevent damage and morbidity.
keyPoints: Red flag diagnoses include septic arthritis, acute crystal-induced arthritis (gout), and fracture.
These conditions present with acute onset of monoarticular or focal musculoskeletal pain.
Prompt diagnosis is essential to avoid damage and morbidity.
items.reference: Approach to Articular and Musculoskeletal Disorders · Approach to Articular and Musculoskeletal Disorders

items.subtopic: Approach to Articular and Musculoskeletal Disorders
items.title: Characterizing Musculoskeletal Complaints for Differential Diagnosis
items.content: A thorough history and comprehensive physical examination can diagnose most musculoskeletal complaints. The evaluation should determine if the complaint is articular or nonarticular, inflammatory or noninflammatory, acute or chronic, and localized (monoarticular) or widespread (polyarticular). This characterization helps narrow diagnostic possibilities.
keyPoints: Most musculoskeletal complaints can be diagnosed with a thorough history and physical examination.
Evaluate if the complaint is articular or nonarticular, inflammatory or noninflammatory, acute or chronic, and localized or widespread.
Characterization aids in narrowing differential diagnoses.
items.reference: Approach to Articular and Musculoskeletal Disorders · Approach to Articular and Musculoskeletal Disorders

items.title: Distinguishing Articular from Nonarticular Pain
items.content: Articular pain is characterized by deep or diffuse pain, limited range of motion in both active and passive movements, swelling, crepitation, instability, locking, or deformity. In contrast, nonarticular disorders present with pain during active movement but not passive, focal tenderness near joints, and physical findings remote from the joint capsule.
keyPoints: Articular: deep/diffuse pain, limited ROM in all movements, swelling, crepitation, instability, locking, deformity
Nonarticular: pain on active movement only, point/focal tenderness, no swelling/crepitus
items.reference: Approach to Articular and Musculoskeletal Disorders · INFLAMMATORY OR NONINFLAMMATORY

items.title: Identifying Inflammatory Disorders
items.content: Inflammatory disorders can be infectious, crystal-induced, immune-related, reactive, or idiopathic. They are suggested by signs of inflammation (erythema, warmth, pain, swelling), systemic symptoms (fever, rash, weight loss), and lab evidence (elevated ESR/CRP, thrombocytosis, anemia, hypoalbuminemia). Chronic stiffness that improves with activity is typical.
keyPoints: Inflammatory signs: erythema, warmth, pain, swelling
Systemic symptoms: fever, rash, weight loss
Lab evidence: elevated ESR/CRP, thrombocytosis, anemia, hypoalbuminemia
Chronic stiffness improves with activity
items.reference: Approach to Articular and Musculoskeletal Disorders · INFLAMMATORY OR NONINFLAMMATORY

items.title: Characteristics of Noninflammatory Disorders
items.content: Noninflammatory disorders include trauma, repetitive use, degeneration, neoplasm, or pain amplification. They are characterized by pain without synovial swelling/warmth, absence of inflammatory/systemic features, intermittent gel phenomena, and normal/negative lab results.
keyPoints: Causes: trauma, repetitive use, degeneration, neoplasm, pain amplification
Characteristics: pain without swelling/warmth, no systemic features
Intermittent gel phenomena, normal/negative labs
items.reference: Approach to Articular and Musculoskeletal Disorders · INFLAMMATORY OR NONINFLAMMATORY

items.title: Understanding Joint Involvement Patterns in Arthropathies
items.content: The distribution and number of joints involved can help differentiate between various arthropathies. Monoarticular or oligoarticular conditions often suggest crystal-induced or infectious arthritis, while polyarticular involvement is more characteristic of OA and RA.
keyPoints: Monoarticular: one joint
Oligoarticular/pauciarticular: two to three joints
Polyarticular: four or more joints
Crystal and infectious arthritis are often mono- or oligoarticular
OA and RA typically present as polyarticular
items.reference: Approach to Articular and Musculoskeletal Disorders · CLINICAL HISTORY

items.title: Classifying Musculoskeletal Disorders by Symptom Duration
items.content: Musculoskeletal disorders can be classified based on the duration of symptoms into acute (≤6 weeks) or chronic (>6 weeks). Acute conditions are often infectious, crystal-induced, or reactive, while chronic conditions may include noninflammatory or immune-mediated diseases.
keyPoints: Acute arthropathies: ≤6 weeks
Chronic conditions: >6 weeks
Acute conditions: infectious, crystal-induced, or reactive
Chronic conditions: OA, RA, polymyositis
items.reference: Approach to Articular and Musculoskeletal Disorders · CLINICAL HISTORY

items.subtopic: Arthralgias | Myalgias/myopathy | Drug-induced dermatomyositis | Tendon rupture/tendinitis | Drug-induced enthesitis | Gout | Drug-induced lupus | Drug-induced subacute lupus | Osteonecrosis/atypical fractures | Osteopenia | Psoriasis | Systemic sclerosis (scleroderma) | Raynaud's phenomenon | Vasculitis
items.title: Common Drugs Associated with Musculoskeletal Disorders
items.content: Various drugs are implicated in causing musculoskeletal disorders, including arthralgias, myalgias/myopathy, and more. Awareness of these associations is crucial for diagnosis and management.
keyPoints: Quinidine, cimetidine, beta blockers, quinolones, chronic acyclovir, interferons, IL-2, nicardipine, vaccines, rifabutin, aromatase inhibitors, HIV protease inhibitors, DPP-4 inhibitors (sitagliptin, linagliptin, alogliptin), paclitaxel, checkpoint inhibitors (ipilimumab, pembrolizumab, nivolumab, atezolizumab, durvalumab, cemiplimab), denosumab, teriparatide, abaloparatide, voriconazole are associated with arthralgias.
Glucocorticoids, penicillamine, hydroxychloroquine, AZT, statins, protease inhibitors, checkpoint inhibitors can cause myalgias/myopathy.
Hydroxyurea, statins, immune checkpoint inhibitors, trastuzumab are linked to drug-induced dermatomyositis.
Quinolones, glucocorticoids, isotretinoin, statins, collagenase injections may lead to tendon rupture/tendinitis.
Dupilumab, voriconazole, fluoroquinolones, retinoids can induce enthesitis.
Diuretics, aspirin, cytotoxics, alcohol are known triggers for gout.
items.reference: Approach to Articular and Musculoskeletal Disorders · Arthralgias | Myalgias/myopathy | Drug-induced dermatomyositis | Tendon rupture/tendinitis | Drug-induced enthesitis | Gout | Drug-induced lupus | Drug-induced subacute lupus | Osteonecrosis/atypical fractures | Osteopenia | Psoriasis | Systemic sclerosis (scleroderma) | Raynaud's phenomenon | Vasculitis

items.subtopic: Arthralgias | Myalgias/myopathy | Drug-induced dermatomyositis | Tendon rupture/tendinitis | Drug-induced enthesitis | Gout | Drug-induced lupus | Drug-induced subacute lupus | Osteonecrosis/atypical fractures | Osteopenia | Psoriasis | Systemic sclerosis (scleroderma) | Raynaud's phenomenon | Vasculitis
items.title: Systemic Features and Comorbidities in Musculoskeletal Disorders
items.content: Musculoskeletal disorders can present with systemic features and are influenced by comorbid conditions, aiding in diagnosis.
keyPoints: Fever may indicate Still's disease or infection.
Rash is associated with SLE or psoriatic arthritis.
Nail abnormalities suggest psoriatic or reactive arthritis.
Myalgias can be due to fibromyalgia or drug-induced myopathy.
Weakness might signal polymyositis or neuropathy.
Comorbidities like diabetes mellitus, renal insufficiency, and osteoporosis have musculoskeletal consequences.
items.reference: Approach to Articular and Musculoskeletal Disorders · Arthralgias | Myalgias/myopathy | Drug-induced dermatomyositis | Tendon rupture/tendinitis | Drug-induced enthesitis | Gout | Drug-induced lupus | Drug-induced subacute lupus | Osteonecrosis/atypical fractures | Osteopenia | Psoriasis | Systemic sclerosis (scleroderma) | Raynaud's phenomenon | Vasculitis

items.title: Early Consideration of Fibromyalgia in the Elderly to Prevent Unnecessary Investigations
items.content: In elderly patients presenting with musculoskeletal complaints, early consideration of fibromyalgia can prevent unnecessary investigations and treatments. This is crucial as fibromyalgia often masquerades as other conditions due to its characteristic widespread aches and pains without significant abnormalities on clinical examination or laboratory testing.
keyPoints: Fibromyalgia affects over 5 million Americans.
Characterized by sleep disturbance, exaggerated pain sensitivity, and multiple symptoms with few physical findings.
Tender 'trigger points' may be elicited in specific areas such as epicondyles, trochanteric bursae, anserine bursae, gluteal, trapezius, and supraspinatus muscles.
items.reference: Approach to Articular and Musculoskeletal Disorders · FIBROMYALGIA | THE ELDERLY PATIENT

items.title: Challenges in Diagnosing Musculoskeletal Disorders in the Elderly
items.content: Diagnosing musculoskeletal disorders in elderly patients is challenging due to the insidious nature of symptoms, comorbidities, and diminished reliability of laboratory tests. Conditions like elevated ESR and low-titer positive RF or ANAs can be misleading, necessitating careful evaluation to avoid misdiagnosis.
keyPoints: 58% of those >65 years will have joint complaints.
ESR may be misleadingly elevated with age.
Low-titer positive tests for rheumatoid factor (RF) and antinuclear antibodies (ANAs) can occur in up to 15% of elderly patients.
items.reference: Approach to Articular and Musculoskeletal Disorders · FIBROMYALGIA | THE ELDERLY PATIENT

items.title: Recognizing Red Flags in Acute Monoarticular Inflammatory Arthritis
items.content: Acute monoarticular inflammatory arthritis is a critical presentation that may indicate severe conditions such as septic arthritis, gout, or pseudogout. These require immediate attention and potentially hospitalization if infection is suspected.
keyPoints: Red flag condition: acute monoarticular inflammatory arthritis
Potential diagnoses include septic arthritis, gout, and pseudogout
May necessitate arthrocentesis and hospitalization if infection is suspected
items.reference: Approach to Articular and Musculoskeletal Disorders · THE HOSPITALIZED PATIENT

items.title: Approach to Febrile Multisystem Disorders in Rheumatic Patients
items.content: In hospitalized rheumatic patients presenting with febrile, multisystem disorders, it's crucial to exclude crystal-induced (gout, pseudogout), infectious, or neoplastic etiologies. The evaluation should focus on the dominant symptom/finding for specificity.
keyPoints: Febrile conditions include vasculitis (PMR, giant cell arteritis, GPA), adult-onset Still's disease, SLE, antiphospholipid syndrome, IgG4-related disease, and sarcoidosis
Exclude crystal-induced, infectious, or neoplastic etiologies
Focus evaluation on the dominant symptom/finding with greatest specificity
items.reference: Approach to Articular and Musculoskeletal Disorders · THE HOSPITALIZED PATIENT

items.title: Quantifying Joint Involvement in Musculoskeletal Disorders
items.content: The examination of 28 easily palpated joints helps quantify the number of tender or swollen joints involved, providing a systematic approach to assessing musculoskeletal disorders.
keyPoints: Examination includes proximal interphalangeals (PIPs), metacarpophalangeals (MCPs), wrists, elbows, shoulders, and knees.
Quantify the number of tender or swollen joints on a scale from 0 to 28.
items.reference: Approach to Articular and Musculoskeletal Disorders · MUSCULOSKELETAL EXAMINATION

items.title: Distinguishing Articular from Nonarticular Swelling
items.content: Differentiating true articular swelling from nonarticular involvement is crucial for accurate diagnosis and treatment planning.
keyPoints: True articular swelling can be caused by bony hypertrophy, synovial effusion or proliferation.
Nonarticular involvement extends beyond normal joint margins.
Synovial effusion presents as fluctuant swelling; synovial hypertrophy feels grape-like and compressible; bony hypertrophy is firm.
items.reference: Approach to Articular and Musculoskeletal Disorders · MUSCULOSKELETAL EXAMINATION

items.title: Assessing Muscle Strength in Musculoskeletal Disorders
items.content: Muscle strength assessment using a 5-point scale aids in identifying the extent of muscle involvement and potential underlying conditions.
keyPoints: Grade muscle strength on a scale from 0 to 5: 0 for no movement, 1 for trace movement or twitch, 2 for movement with gravity eliminated, 3 for movement against gravity only, 4 for movement against gravity and resistance, and 5 for normal strength.
Observe patient performance in tasks like walking, rising from a chair, grasping, and writing.
items.reference: Approach to Articular and Musculoskeletal Disorders · MUSCULOSKELETAL EXAMINATION

items.title: Distinguishing Hand Pain Etiologies: Trauma vs. Systemic Causes
items.content: Focal hand pain can arise from various causes such as trauma, overuse, infection, or arthritis. Bilateral hand complaints often indicate systemic or inflammatory conditions like osteoarthritis (OA) or rheumatoid arthritis (RA). Recognizing the pattern of joint involvement aids in differentiating these etiologies.
keyPoints: Focal unilateral hand pain may result from trauma, overuse, infection, or reactive/crystal-induced arthritis.
Bilateral hand complaints suggest degenerative (e.g., OA), systemic, or inflammatory/immune (e.g., RA) causes.
OA manifests as DIP and PIP joint pain with Heberden's and Bouchard's nodes; thumb base involvement also suggests OA.
RA involves symmetric polyarticular arthritis of the PIP, MCP, intercarpal, and carpometacarpal joints.
items.reference: Approach to Articular and Musculoskeletal Disorders · REGIONAL RHEUMATIC COMPLAINTS | HAND PAIN

items.title: Identifying Dactylitis: Causes and Presentation
items.content: Dactylitis is characterized by soft tissue swelling of an entire digit, often presenting with a sausage-like appearance. It can be caused by conditions such as psoriatic arthritis, spondyloarthritis, or sickle cell disease.
keyPoints: Dactylitis manifests as soft tissue swelling of the whole digit, resembling a 'sausage'.
Common causes include psoriatic arthritis, spondyloarthritis, juvenile spondylitis, and sickle cell disease.
items.reference: Approach to Articular and Musculoskeletal Disorders · REGIONAL RHEUMATIC COMPLAINTS | HAND PAIN

items.title: Diagnosing Carpal Tunnel Syndrome: Clinical Tests and Associations
items.content: Carpal tunnel syndrome results from median nerve compression within the carpal tunnel, leading to wrist pain and paresthesia. It is diagnosed using Durkan's test or Tinel's sign, with confirmation often requiring nerve conduction velocity testing.
keyPoints: Carpal tunnel syndrome causes wrist pain radiating to thumb, second, third, and radial half of fourth finger.
Associated conditions include pregnancy, edema, trauma, OA, inflammatory arthritis, and infiltrative disorders like amyloidosis.
Durkan's test and Tinel's sign are used for diagnosis; nerve conduction velocity testing may be needed due to low sensitivity.
items.reference: Approach to Articular and Musculoskeletal Disorders · REGIONAL RHEUMATIC COMPLAINTS | HAND PAIN

items.title: Differentiating Glenohumeral Pain from Periarticular Processes
items.content: Glenohumeral pain is typically elicited by specific movements, whereas periarticular processes are suggested by pain during active (not passive) motion. This distinction helps in identifying the source of shoulder pain.
keyPoints: Pain with movement in all planes suggests arthritis.
Active motion pain indicates a periarticular process.
items.reference: Approach to Articular and Musculoskeletal Disorders · SHOULDER PAIN

items.title: Identifying Rotator Cuff Pathology
items.content: Rotator cuff tendinitis or tears are common in the elderly and active individuals, often presenting with pain on active abduction and positive impingement signs. MRI or ultrasound confirms the diagnosis.
keyPoints: Nearly 30% of elderly with shoulder pain have rotator cuff issues.
Neer test: Pain before $180^{	ext{o}}$ indicates impingement.
Drop arm test: Positive if patient cannot hold or lower arm slowly.
items.reference: Approach to Articular and Musculoskeletal Disorders · SHOULDER PAIN

items.title: Recognizing Fibromyalgia in Shoulder Pain
items.content: Fibromyalgia should be suspected when glenohumeral pain is accompanied by diffuse periarticular pain, tender points, and sleep disturbance. This helps differentiate it from other causes of shoulder pain.
keyPoints: Glenohumeral pain with diffuse periarticular pain suggests fibromyalgia.
Presence of tender points (e.g., trapezius or supraspinatus).
Associated sleep disturbances.
items.reference: Approach to Articular and Musculoskeletal Disorders · SHOULDER PAIN

items.title: Detecting Synovial Effusions in Knee Pain
items.content: Synovial effusions can be detected by specific maneuvers that identify fluid accumulation in the knee joint, which is crucial for diagnosing conditions like OA and RA.
keyPoints: Balloting the patella downward or eliciting a 'bulge sign' helps detect synovial effusions.
Manual compression of synovial fluid from the suprapatellar pouch can reveal small to moderate effusions (<100 mL).
These maneuvers are effective for identifying fluid in conditions like OA and RA.
items.reference: Approach to Articular and Musculoskeletal Disorders · KNEE PAIN

items.title: Identifying Meniscal Tears through Clinical Examination
items.content: Meniscal tears can present with specific symptoms and physical examination findings, such as pain during certain movements or a positive McMurray test.
keyPoints: Symptoms like 'locking,' 'buckling,' or 'giving way' suggest meniscal damage.
Pain on palpation over the joint line or stress testing may indicate a tear.
A positive McMurray test, with pain and clicking during knee rotation, suggests lateral or medial meniscus tears.
items.reference: Approach to Articular and Musculoskeletal Disorders · KNEE PAIN

items.title: Assessing Cruciate Ligament Integrity in Acute Knee Pain
items.content: Cruciate ligament injuries can be suspected based on acute pain, swelling, and specific physical examination tests like the drawer sign.
keyPoints: Acute onset of knee pain with swelling may indicate cruciate ligament damage.
A positive anterior or posterior drawer test suggests anterior or posterior cruciate ligament injury, respectively.
Contralateral comparison enhances detection of significant tibial movement.
items.reference: Approach to Articular and Musculoskeletal Disorders · KNEE PAIN

items.title: Understanding Hip Pain Localization and Differentiation via Telemedicine
items.content: Hip pain is often mislocalized to the posterior gluteal region, but true hip joint pain has distinct characteristics. In telehealth evaluations, observing gait and range of motion can help differentiate between conditions like sciatica, trochanteric bursitis, and iliopsoas bursitis.
keyPoints: Hip pain often localized to posterior gluteal musculature with radiation down the posterolateral thigh.
Sciatica involves unilateral neuropathic pain from L4-S1 nerve impingement.
Trochanteric bursitis diagnosed by point tenderness over trochanteric bursa; swelling and warmth usually absent.
True hip joint pain is anterior, over the inguinal ligament, radiates medially to groin, worsened by adduction/rotation.
Iliopsoas bursitis mimics true hip pain, localized to groin/anterior thigh, worsens with hyperextension.
items.reference: Approach to Articular and Musculoskeletal Disorders · HIP PAIN | TELEHEALTH EVALUATION

items.title: Effective Telehealth Evaluation for Hip Pain
items.content: Telemedicine can effectively assess hip pain through functional observation and patient self-examination. Key components include evaluating gait, range of motion, and specific maneuvers to identify abnormalities.
keyPoints: Telemedicine requires quality audio/video connections; suitable for routine assessments.

Televideo exam should assess: (1) gait, (2) rising from seated position, (3) cervical range of motion, (4) upper extremity by contralateral comparison, (5) side-by-side inspection of knees, ankles, feet.
Patient education and training on technology use are crucial for effective telehealth visits.
items.reference: Approach to Articular and Musculoskeletal Disorders · HIP PAIN | TELEHEALTH EVALUATION

items.title: When to Order Laboratory Tests in Musculoskeletal Disorders
items.content: Laboratory tests should be ordered for musculoskeletal disorders when specific clinical features suggest the need, such as monoarticular presentations, traumatic or inflammatory conditions, neurologic findings, systemic manifestations, or chronic symptoms lasting more than 6 weeks without response to symptomatic measures.
keyPoints: Indicated for monoarticular presentations, traumatic/inflammatory conditions, neurologic/systemic findings, and chronic symptoms (>6 weeks) unresponsive to treatment.
Laboratory tests should confirm a specific clinical diagnosis rather than screen for vague rheumatic complaints.
Avoid indiscriminate use of broad diagnostic test batteries; focus on clinically indicated investigations.
items.reference: Approach to Articular and Musculoskeletal Disorders · LABORATORY INVESTIGATIONS (part 1)

items.title: Interpreting Acute-Phase Reactants and Serum Uric Acid in Musculoskeletal Disorders
items.content: Acute-phase reactants like ESR or CRP help differentiate inflammatory from noninflammatory disorders. Extreme elevations suggest serious illness, but not all active conditions will show elevated levels. Serum uric acid is crucial for diagnosing gout and monitoring therapy response.
keyPoints: ESR/CRP are useful in discriminating between inflammatory and noninflammatory disorders; extreme elevation suggests serious illness (e.g., sepsis, PMR).
Not all active SLE or RA patients will have elevated ESR/CRP; nearly 50% may show normal results.
Serum uric acid levels: men (4.0–8.6 mg/dL), women (3.0–5.9 mg/dL); target <6 mg/dL for gout management, <5 mg/dL if tophi are present.
items.reference: Approach to Articular and Musculoskeletal Disorders · LABORATORY INVESTIGATIONS (part 1)

items.title: When to Order Specific Serologic Tests
items.content: Serologic tests for RF, CCP antibodies, ANAs, and others should be ordered based on clinical evidence suggesting a specific diagnosis due to their poor predictive value in low pretest probability scenarios.
keyPoints: Serologic tests have poor predictive value when used for screening with low pretest probability.
4–5% of healthy individuals may test positive for RF and ANAs, but only 1% and <0.4% will have RA or SLE respectively.
IgM RF is found in 80% of RA patients; CCP antibodies are more specific than RF.
items.reference: Approach to Articular and Musculoskeletal Disorders · LABORATORY INVESTIGATIONS (part 2)

items.title: Interpreting ANA Test Results
items.content: ANA tests are sensitive but not specific for lupus, with interpretation depending on titer magnitude and immunofluorescence pattern.
keyPoints: Positive ANAs found in nearly all SLE patients; also seen in other autoimmune diseases and conditions like thyroid disease.
5% of adults have positive ANAs, up to 15% in elderly or chronically ill individuals, and up to 80% with thyroid disease.
Peripheral pattern on ANA test is highly specific for lupus.
items.reference: Approach to Articular and Musculoskeletal Disorders · LABORATORY INVESTIGATIONS (part 2)

items.title: Synovial Fluid Analysis in Arthritis
items.content: Aspiration and analysis of synovial fluid are crucial in acute monoarthritis or suspected infectious/crystal-induced arthropathy to distinguish between inflammatory and noninflammatory processes.
keyPoints: Normal synovial fluid is clear, viscous with <2000 WBC/μL; inflammatory fluid has >2000 WBC/μL and reduced viscosity.
Septic arthritis typically shows a WBC count >50,000/μL and low viscosity.
Monosodium urate crystals in gout are needle-shaped and negatively birefringent under polarized microscopy.
items.reference: Approach to Articular and Musculoskeletal Disorders · LABORATORY INVESTIGATIONS (part 2)

items.title: When to Use Conventional Radiography in Musculoskeletal Disorders
items.content: Conventional radiography is most appropriate for diagnosing articular disorders when there's a history of trauma, suspected chronic infection, progressive disability, or monoarticular involvement. It is also useful when therapeutic alterations are considered or for baseline assessment of chronic processes.
keyPoints: Useful in cases with trauma, chronic infection, progressive disability, or monoarticular involvement
Helps in therapeutic decision-making and baseline assessments
Early radiography may only show soft tissue swelling or juxtaarticular demineralization in acute arthritis
items.reference: Approach to Articular and Musculoskeletal Disorders · DIAGNOSTIC IMAGING (part 1) | DIAGNOSTIC IMAGING (part 2)

items.title: Advantages of MRI Over Other Imaging Modalities
items.content: MRI is preferred for evaluating musculoskeletal structures due to its ability to provide multiplanar images with fine anatomic detail and contrast resolution. It excels in visualizing fascia, vessels, nerve, muscle, cartilage, ligaments, tendons, pannus, synovial effusions, and bone marrow.
keyPoints: Provides multiplanar images with detailed anatomical visualization
Superior for assessing soft tissue injuries, intraarticular derangements, and spinal cord or nerve root damage
Preferred over arthrography or CT in complex musculoskeletal evaluations
items.reference: Approach to Articular and Musculoskeletal Disorders · DIAGNOSTIC IMAGING (part 1) | DIAGNOSTIC IMAGING (part 2)

items.title: Role of Advanced Imaging Techniques in Musculoskeletal Disorders
items.content: Advanced imaging techniques like CT and MRI are used when conventional radiography is inadequate. CT provides detailed visualization of the axial skeleton, while MRI offers superior soft tissue resolution.
keyPoints: CT useful for evaluating low back pain, sacroiliitis, osteoid osteoma, and stress fractures
MRI revolutionized imaging with its ability to visualize complex musculoskeletal structures
PET/CT scans are used in metastatic evaluations for better anatomic localization
items.reference: Approach to Articular and Musculoskeletal Disorders · DIAGNOSTIC IMAGING (part 1) | DIAGNOSTIC IMAGING (part 2)

items.subtopic: Approach to Articular and Musculoskeletal Disorders
items.question: Which of the following conditions is NOT considered a 'red flag' diagnosis that requires prompt identification?
items.explanation: 'Red flag' diagnoses include septic arthritis, gout, and fracture.
items.reference: Approach to Articular and Musculoskeletal Disorders · Approach to Articular and Musculoskeletal Disorders

items.subtopic: Approach to Articular and Musculoskeletal Disorders
items.question: What is the primary goal of musculoskeletal evaluation?
options: To prescribe immediate treatment for all conditions
To establish a differential diagnosis leading to accurate diagnosis and timely therapy
To diagnose every musculoskeletal complaint as arthritis
items.explanation: The goal is to formulate a differential diagnosis that leads to an accurate diagnosis and timely therapy.
items.reference: Approach to Articular and Musculoskeletal Disorders · Approach to Articular and Musculoskeletal Disorders

items.subtopic: Approach to Articular and Musculoskeletal Disorders
items.question: Which of the following best describes arthralgia?
options: Joint pain that is synonymous with arthritis
Common symptom that may indicate arthritis but does not imply pathology
items.explanation: Arthralgia refers to joint pain and is common, but it is not synonymous with arthritis.
items.reference: Approach to Articular and Musculoskeletal Disorders · Approach to Articular and Musculoskeletal Disorders

items.question: Which of the following is a characteristic feature that helps distinguish inflammatory disorders from noninflammatory conditions?
options: Pain without synovial swelling or warmth
Deep or diffuse pain with limited range of motion
items.explanation: Inflammatory disorders are characterized by deep or diffuse pain, limited range of motion, and swelling.
items.reference: Approach to Articular and Musculoskeletal Disorders · INFLAMMATORY OR NONINFLAMMATORY

items.question: What is a typical feature of noninflammatory musculoskeletal conditions?
options: Prolonged stiffness that improves with activity
Point or focal tenderness in regions adjacent to articular structures
items.explanation: Noninflammatory conditions often demonstrate point or focal tenderness and lack systemic inflammatory features.
items.reference: Approach to Articular and Musculoskeletal Disorders · INFLAMMATORY OR NONINFLAMMATORY

items.question: Which of the following is NOT typically associated with noninflammatory musculoskeletal disorders?
options: Pain without synovial swelling or warmth
items.explanation: Noninflammatory disorders are characterized by the absence of elevated CRP, a marker of inflammation.
items.reference: Approach to Articular and Musculoskeletal Disorders · INFLAMMATORY OR NONINFLAMMATORY

items.question: What is a common feature of inflammatory musculoskeletal conditions?
items.explanation: Inflammatory disorders often have stiffness that is prolonged and improves with activity.
items.reference: Approach to Articular and Musculoskeletal Disorders · INFLAMMATORY OR NONINFLAMMATORY

items.question: Which condition is more likely to present with an abrupt onset?
items.explanation: An abrupt onset is typical of septic arthritis or gout.
items.reference: Approach to Articular and Musculoskeletal Disorders · CLINICAL HISTORY

items.question: Which disorder is commonly associated with familial aggregation?
items.explanation: Familial aggregation may be seen with ankylosing spondylitis, gout, and Heberden's nodes of OA.
items.reference: Approach to Articular and Musculoskeletal Disorders · CLINICAL HISTORY

items.question: Which type of arthritis is more common in men?
items.explanation: Gout, spondyloarthritis, and ankylosing spondylitis are more common in men.
items.reference: Approach to Articular and Musculoskeletal Disorders · CLINICAL HISTORY

items.question: Which of the following drugs is associated with both drug-induced lupus and vasculitis?
items.explanation: Hydralazine is associated with both drug-induced lupus and vasculitis.

items.question: Which medication is implicated in both gout and osteoporosis-related complications?
items.explanation: Glucocorticoids are associated with osteonecrosis (a complication of osteoporosis) and can also lead to gout.

items.question: Which of the following drugs is associated with both tendon rupture and myopathy?
items.explanation: Quinolones are associated with both tendon rupture and myopathy.

items.question: Which of the following is a common comorbidity associated with fibromyalgia?
items.explanation: Fibromyalgia often coexists with numerous comorbidities including irritable bowel syndrome.
items.reference: Approach to Articular and Musculoskeletal Disorders · FIBROMYALGIA | THE ELDERLY PATIENT

items.question: What is a characteristic feature of fibromyalgia?
items.explanation: Fibromyalgia is characterized by widespread aches and pains.
items.reference: Approach to Articular and Musculoskeletal Disorders · FIBROMYALGIA | THE ELDERLY PATIENT

items.question: Which of the following symptoms may be elicited in fibromyalgia patients?
items.explanation: Tender 'trigger points' may be elicited over specific areas in fibromyalgia.
items.reference: Approach to Articular and Musculoskeletal Disorders · FIBROMYALGIA | THE ELDERLY PATIENT

items.question: Which of the following is NOT a typical reason for hospital admission in patients with rheumatic disorders?
options: Exacerbation of an existing musculoskeletal disorder
items.explanation: Rheumatic patients need not be admitted for widespread pain alone.
items.reference: Approach to Articular and Musculoskeletal Disorders · THE HOSPITALIZED PATIENT

items.question: What is a 'red flag' presentation in hospitalized rheumatic patients that may require arthrocentesis?
options: Exacerbation of an existing musculoskeletal disorder
New medical diagnoses like thrombotic events
items.explanation: Acute monoarticular inflammatory arthritis is a 'red flag' presentation.
items.reference: Approach to Articular and Musculoskeletal Disorders · THE HOSPITALIZED PATIENT

items.question: Which of the following conditions should be considered in febrile, multisystem disorders in rheumatic patients?
options: Vasculitis such as PMR or giant cell arteritis
items.explanation: Febrile conditions like vasculitis should be considered.
items.reference: Approach to Articular and Musculoskeletal Disorders · THE HOSPITALIZED PATIENT

items.question: Which of the following is NOT a method used to assess joint stability?
options: Stabilizing the proximal joint and applying manual stress to the distal appendage in different planes.
Palpating for synovial effusion or hypertrophy.
Assessing passive range of motion with contralateral comparison.
items.explanation: Palpating for synovial effusion or hypertrophy is used to distinguish types of swelling, not joint stability.
items.reference: Approach to Articular and Musculoskeletal Disorders · MUSCULOSKELETAL EXAMINATION

items.question: What does the 'bulge sign' help identify in a musculoskeletal examination?
items.explanation: The 'bulge sign' is used to identify small to moderate knee effusions, indicating synovial effusion.
items.reference: Approach to Articular and Musculoskeletal Disorders · MUSCULOSKELETAL EXAMINATION

items.question: What does a goniometer measure during a musculoskeletal examination?
items.explanation: A goniometer is used to accurately quantify the arc of movement in joints during a musculoskeletal examination.
items.reference: Approach to Articular and Musculoskeletal Disorders · MUSCULOSKELETAL EXAMINATION

items.question: Which condition may be indicated by passive motion exceeding active motion?
options: Periarticular process such as tendinitis or myopathy.
items.explanation: If passive motion exceeds active motion, a periarticular process like tendinitis or tendon rupture should be considered.
items.reference: Approach to Articular and Musculoskeletal Disorders · MUSCULOSKELETAL EXAMINATION

items.question: Which type of arthritis is most likely associated with Heberden's and Bouchard's nodes?
items.explanation: Heberden's and Bouchard's nodes are characteristic of Osteoarthritis.
items.reference: Approach to Articular and Musculoskeletal Disorders · REGIONAL RHEUMATIC COMPLAINTS | HAND PAIN

items.question: What is the typical presentation of dactylitis?
options: Pain in the distal interphalangeal joints
Soft tissue swelling of an entire digit with a sausage-like appearance
Bony hypertrophy at the second and third MCP joints
items.explanation: Dactylitis presents as soft tissue swelling of the whole digit, often described as sausage-like.
items.reference: Approach to Articular and Musculoskeletal Disorders · REGIONAL RHEUMATIC COMPLAINTS | HAND PAIN

items.question: Which test is used to diagnose de Quervain's tenosynovitis?
items.explanation: De Quervain's tenosynovitis is diagnosed using Finkelstein's test.
items.reference: Approach to Articular and Musculoskeletal Disorders · REGIONAL RHEUMATIC COMPLAINTS | HAND PAIN

items.question: Which disorder is suggested by pain and paresthesia radiating to the thumb, second, third, and radial half of the fourth finger?
items.explanation: Carpal tunnel syndrome presents with pain and paresthesia in the median nerve distribution.
items.reference: Approach to Articular and Musculoskeletal Disorders · REGIONAL RHEUMATIC COMPLAINTS | HAND PAIN

items.question: Which condition should be suspected when glenohumeral pain is accompanied by diffuse periarticular pain, tender points, and sleep disturbance?
items.explanation: The passage indicates that fibromyalgia should be suspected when glenohumeral pain is accompanied by diffuse periarticular pain, tender points, and sleep disturbance.
items.reference: Approach to Articular and Musculoskeletal Disorders · SHOULDER PAIN

items.question: What test involves raising the patient's arm into forced flexion while stabilizing and preventing rotation of the scapula to diagnose impingement?
items.explanation: The Neer test is described as raising the patient's arm into forced flexion while stabilizing and preventing rotation of the scapula to diagnose impingement.
items.reference: Approach to Articular and Musculoskeletal Disorders · SHOULDER PAIN

items.question: Which tendon is best identified by palpating it in its groove during internal and external rotation of the humerus?
items.explanation: The passage states that the bicipital tendon is best identified by palpating it in its groove during internal and external rotation of the humerus.
items.reference: Approach to Articular and Musculoskeletal Disorders · SHOULDER PAIN

items.question: What condition is suggested by pain on active abduction, but not passive abduction, and night pain?
items.explanation: The passage indicates that rotator cuff tendinitis is suggested by pain on active abduction (but not passive), night pain, and evidence of impingement signs.
items.reference: Approach to Articular and Musculoskeletal Disorders · SHOULDER PAIN

items.question: Which knee malalignment results from asymmetric cartilage loss medially?
items.explanation: Genu valgum (knock-knees) results from asymmetric cartilage loss medially.
items.reference: Approach to Articular and Musculoskeletal Disorders · KNEE PAIN

items.question: What is the most effective method to detect small to moderate knee effusions (<100 mL)?
items.explanation: The 'milk test' is effective in detecting small to moderate knee effusions.
items.reference: Approach to Articular and Musculoskeletal Disorders · KNEE PAIN

items.question: Which condition may present with a painful click during inward rotation of the leg when performing the McMurray test?
items.explanation: A painful click during inward rotation in the McMurray test indicates a lateral meniscus tear.
items.reference: Approach to Articular and Musculoskeletal Disorders · KNEE PAIN

items.question: What is often palpated with the knee partially flexed and best viewed posteriorly?
items.explanation: A Baker's cyst is often palpated with the knee partially flexed and best viewed posteriorly.
items.reference: Approach to Articular and Musculoskeletal Disorders · KNEE PAIN

items.question: Which nerve roots are commonly involved in sciatica that presents with unilateral neuropathic pain extending from the gluteal region down the posterolateral leg to the foot?
items.explanation: Sciatica is caused by impingement of the L4, L5, or S1 nerve roots.
items.reference: Approach to Articular and Musculoskeletal Disorders · HIP PAIN | TELEHEALTH EVALUATION

items.question: Where is pain from trochanteric bursitis typically localized?
options: Lateral area overlying the trochanteric bursa
items.explanation: Trochanteric pain is localized laterally to the area overlying the trochanteric bursa.
items.reference: Approach to Articular and Musculoskeletal Disorders · HIP PAIN | TELEHEALTH EVALUATION

items.question: What is a common characteristic of iliopsoas bursitis that differentiates it from true hip joint pain?
options: Pain worsens with hip flexion and external rotation
Pain is located posteriorly over the gluteal region
Swelling and warmth are usually present
Pain radiates medially to the groin
items.explanation: Iliopsoas bursitis pain tends to worsen with hyperextension of the hip, and patients often prefer to flex and externally rotate the hip.
items.reference: Approach to Articular and Musculoskeletal Disorders · HIP PAIN | TELEHEALTH EVALUATION

items.question: In which of the following scenarios is laboratory evaluation indicated according to the passage?
options: All patients with joint pain regardless of duration or severity
Patients with vague rheumatic complaints without specific clinical diagnosis
Routine screening in all musculoskeletal disorders
items.explanation: Laboratory evaluation is indicated for monoarticular presentations, among other specified conditions.
items.reference: Approach to Articular and Musculoskeletal Disorders · LABORATORY INVESTIGATIONS (part 1)

items.question: Which of the following statements about acute-phase reactants ESR and CRP is correct?
options: They are only elevated in autoimmune disorders
Both can be elevated with infection, inflammation, or neoplasia
An extreme elevation is always indicative of a minor illness
All active inflammatory conditions will have an elevated ESR or CRP
items.explanation: ESR and CRP can be elevated in various conditions including infection, inflammation, autoimmune disorders, neoplasia, etc.
items.reference: Approach to Articular and Musculoskeletal Disorders · LABORATORY INVESTIGATIONS (part 1)

items.question: What is the target goal for serum urate levels when monitoring response to urate-lowering therapy?
options: <6 mg/dL, or <5 mg/dL if tophi are present
Always >10 mg/dL regardless of clinical presentation
No specific target goal is mentioned
items.explanation: The target serum urate level should be <6 mg/dL, or <5 mg/dL if tophi are present.
items.reference: Approach to Articular and Musculoskeletal Disorders · LABORATORY INVESTIGATIONS (part 1)

items.question: Which serologic test is more specific for rheumatoid arthritis (RA) than IgM RF?
items.explanation: Anti-CCP antibodies are more specific for RA compared to IgM RF.
items.reference: Approach to Articular and Musculoskeletal Disorders · LABORATORY INVESTIGATIONS (part 2)

items.question: What is the typical WBC count range in inflammatory synovial fluid?
items.explanation: Inflammatory synovial fluid typically has a WBC count of 2000–50,000/μL.
items.reference: Approach to Articular and Musculoskeletal Disorders · LABORATORY INVESTIGATIONS (part 2)

items.question: Which pattern observed by immunofluorescence microscopy is highly specific for lupus?
items.explanation: A peripheral, or rim pattern, is highly specific and suggestive of lupus.
items.reference: Approach to Articular and Musculoskeletal Disorders · LABORATORY INVESTIGATIONS (part 2)

items.question: What characteristic feature distinguishes monosodium urate crystals in gout?
items.explanation: Monosodium urate crystals in gout are long, needle-shaped, and negatively birefringent.
items.reference: Approach to Articular and Musculoskeletal Disorders · LABORATORY INVESTIGATIONS (part 2)

items.question: Which imaging modality is most appropriate for evaluating zygapophyseal and sacroiliac joints?
items.explanation: CT provides detailed visualization of the axial skeleton, including articulations like zygapophyseal and sacroiliac joints.
items.reference: Approach to Articular and Musculoskeletal Disorders · DIAGNOSTIC IMAGING (part 1) | DIAGNOSTIC IMAGING (part 2)

items.question: Which imaging technique is primarily used for detecting occult infection or malignancy?
items.explanation: $^{67}$Ga binds to serum and cellular transferrin and lactoferrin, being preferentially taken up by neutrophils, macrophages, bacteria, and tumor tissue.
items.reference: Approach to Articular and Musculoskeletal Disorders · DIAGNOSTIC IMAGING (part 1) | DIAGNOSTIC IMAGING (part 2)

items.question: Which imaging modality has largely replaced scintigraphy in diagnosing osseous infection?
items.explanation: MRI has largely replaced scintigraphy for diagnosing osseous infections, inflammation, and other conditions.
items.reference: Approach to Articular and Musculoskeletal Disorders · DIAGNOSTIC IMAGING (part 1) | DIAGNOSTIC IMAGING (part 2)

items.question: What is the primary advantage of MRI over CT in musculoskeletal imaging?
options: Multiplanar images with fine anatomic detail
items.explanation: MRI provides multiplanar images with fine anatomic detail and contrast resolution, which is advantageous for visualizing various musculoskeletal structures.
items.reference: Approach to Articular and Musculoskeletal Disorders · DIAGNOSTIC IMAGING (part 1) | DIAGNOSTIC IMAGING (part 2)

items.subtopic: Approach to Articular and Musculoskeletal Disorders
items.statement: Most musculoskeletal complaints can be diagnosed without any history or physical examination.
items.explanation: A thorough history and comprehensive physical and musculoskeletal examination are necessary for diagnosis.
items.reference: Approach to Articular and Musculoskeletal Disorders · Approach to Articular and Musculoskeletal Disorders

items.subtopic: Approach to Articular and Musculoskeletal Disorders
items.statement: 'Red flag' conditions must be diagnosed promptly to avoid damage and morbidity.
items.explanation: Prompt diagnosis of 'red flag' conditions like septic arthritis, gout, or fracture is crucial.
items.reference: Approach to Articular and Musculoskeletal Disorders · Approach to Articular and Musculoskeletal Disorders

items.statement: Noninflammatory musculoskeletal conditions are often characterized by swelling from synovial proliferation.
items.explanation: Swelling from synovial proliferation is typically associated with inflammatory disorders.
items.reference: Approach to Articular and Musculoskeletal Disorders · INFLAMMATORY OR NONINFLAMMATORY

items.statement: Gout and pseudogout are examples of crystal-induced inflammatory disorders.
items.explanation: Gout and pseudogout are indeed crystal-induced inflammatory conditions.
items.reference: Approach to Articular and Musculoskeletal Disorders · INFLAMMATORY OR NONINFLAMMATORY

items.statement: Osteoarthritis typically presents with symmetric joint involvement.
items.explanation: OA may be either symmetric or asymmetric and oligo- or polyarticular.
items.reference: Approach to Articular and Musculoskeletal Disorders · CLINICAL HISTORY

items.statement: Rheumatoid arthritis is more prevalent in the elderly than in middle age.
items.explanation: RA is frequent in middle age, while OA and PMR are more prevalent in the elderly.
items.reference: Approach to Articular and Musculoskeletal Disorders · CLINICAL HISTORY

items.subtopic: Arthralgias | Myalgias/myopathy | Drug-induced dermatomyositis | Tendon rupture/tendinitis | Drug-induced enthesitis | Gout | Drug-induced lupus | Drug-induced subacute lupus | Osteonecrosis/atypical fractures | Osteopenia | Psoriasis | Systemic sclerosis (scleroderma) | Raynaud's phenomenon | Vasculitis
items.statement: TNF inhibitors are associated with both psoriasis and systemic sclerosis.
items.explanation: TNF inhibitors can be used in the treatment of psoriasis and have been implicated in triggering systemic sclerosis.
items.reference: Approach to Articular and Musculoskeletal Disorders · Arthralgias | Myalgias/myopathy | Drug-induced dermatomyositis | Tendon rupture/tendinitis | Drug-induced enthesitis | Gout | Drug-induced lupus | Drug-induced subacute lupus | Osteonecrosis/atypical fractures | Osteopenia | Psoriasis | Systemic sclerosis (scleroderma) | Raynaud's phenomenon | Vasculitis

items.subtopic: Arthralgias | Myalgias/myopathy | Drug-induced dermatomyositis | Tendon rupture/tendinitis | Drug-induced enthesitis | Gout | Drug-induced lupus | Drug-induced subacute lupus | Osteonecrosis/atypical fractures | Osteopenia | Psoriasis | Systemic sclerosis (scleroderma) | Raynaud's phenomenon | Vasculitis
items.statement: Rifabutin is associated with drug-induced dermatomyositis.
items.explanation: Rifabutin is not listed as a cause of drug-induced dermatomyositis.
items.reference: Approach to Articular and Musculoskeletal Disorders · Arthralgias | Myalgias/myopathy | Drug-induced dermatomyositis | Tendon rupture/tendinitis | Drug-induced enthesitis | Gout | Drug-induced lupus | Drug-induced subacute lupus | Osteonecrosis/atypical fractures | Osteopenia | Psoriasis | Systemic sclerosis (scleroderma) | Raynaud's phenomenon | Vasculitis

items.statement: Laboratory testing is highly reliable for diagnosing fibromyalgia in the elderly.
items.explanation: Musculoskeletal disorders may be overlooked because of diminished reliability of laboratory testing in the elderly.
items.reference: Approach to Articular and Musculoskeletal Disorders · FIBROMYALGIA | THE ELDERLY PATIENT

items.statement: The incidence of rheumatic diseases decreases with age.
items.explanation: The incidence of rheumatic diseases rises with age, affecting over 58% of those >65 years.
items.reference: Approach to Articular and Musculoskeletal Disorders · FIBROMYALGIA | THE ELDERLY PATIENT

items.statement: Patients with chronic inflammatory disorders are at increased risk of cardiovascular events.
items.explanation: Chronic inflammatory disorders increase the risk of cardiovascular events.
items.reference: Approach to Articular and Musculoskeletal Disorders · THE HOSPITALIZED PATIENT

items.statement: Rheumatic patients are typically admitted to hospital for their autoimmune disorder.
items.explanation: Admission is usually due to comorbid or new medical problems, not the autoimmune disorder itself.
items.reference: Approach to Articular and Musculoskeletal Disorders · THE HOSPITALIZED PATIENT

items.statement: Joint deformity always indicates an acute pathologic process.
items.explanation: Joint deformity usually indicates a long-standing or aggressive pathologic process, not necessarily acute.
items.reference: Approach to Articular and Musculoskeletal Disorders · MUSCULOSKELETAL EXAMINATION

items.statement: Minor joint crepitus during palpation is uncommon and always indicative of significant cartilage degeneration.
items.explanation: Minor joint crepitus is common and only becomes concerning if it indicates significant cartilage degeneration.
items.reference: Approach to Articular and Musculoskeletal Disorders · MUSCULOSKELETAL EXAMINATION

items.statement: Psoriatic arthritis can be distinguished from OA by the presence of inflammation, nail pitting, or onycholysis.
items.explanation: Psoriatic arthritis is differentiated from OA by these inflammatory features.
items.reference: Approach to Articular and Musculoskeletal Disorders · REGIONAL RHEUMATIC COMPLAINTS | HAND PAIN

items.statement: Carpal tunnel syndrome can be definitively diagnosed with Finkelstein's test.
items.explanation: Finkelstein's test is used for de Quervain's tenosynovitis, not carpal tunnel syndrome.
items.reference: Approach to Articular and Musculoskeletal Disorders · REGIONAL RHEUMATIC COMPLAINTS | HAND PAIN

items.statement: Synovial effusion or tissue is commonly palpable in the shoulder.
items.explanation: The passage states that synovial effusion or tissue is seldom palpable in the shoulder.
items.reference: Approach to Articular and Musculoskeletal Disorders · SHOULDER PAIN

items.statement: Rotator cuff tears are uncommon in the elderly with shoulder pain.
items.explanation: The passage mentions that nearly 30% of the elderly with shoulder pain will have rotator cuff tendinitis or tear as a primary cause.
items.reference: Approach to Articular and Musculoskeletal Disorders · SHOULDER PAIN

items.statement: The bulge sign is effective in detecting large synovial effusions.
items.explanation: The bulge sign is only effective for small to moderate effusions (<100 mL).
items.reference: Approach to Articular and Musculoskeletal Disorders · KNEE PAIN

items.statement: Anserine bursitis can be a cause of knee pain in adults.
items.explanation: Anserine bursitis is an often-missed periarticular cause of knee pain.
items.reference: Approach to Articular and Musculoskeletal Disorders · KNEE PAIN

items.statement: Swelling and warmth are usually absent in trochanteric bursitis due to the depth of the bursa.
items.explanation: The depth of the trochanteric bursa typically results in an absence of swelling and warmth.
items.reference: Approach to Articular and Musculoskeletal Disorders · HIP PAIN | TELEHEALTH EVALUATION

items.statement: Pain from the 'true' hip joint is commonly located posteriorly over the gluteal region.
items.explanation: 'True' hip joint pain tends to be located anteriorly, over the inguinal ligament.
items.reference: Approach to Articular and Musculoskeletal Disorders · HIP PAIN | TELEHEALTH EVALUATION

items.statement: Serum uric acid levels always correlate with the severity of gout.
items.explanation: Uric acid levels may not correlate with the severity of gout.
items.reference: Approach to Articular and Musculoskeletal Disorders · LABORATORY INVESTIGATIONS (part 1)

items.statement: Indiscriminate use of broad batteries of diagnostic tests is a useful and cost-effective means to establish a diagnosis.
items.explanation: Broad batteries of diagnostic tests are rarely useful or cost-effective for establishing a diagnosis.
items.reference: Approach to Articular and Musculoskeletal Disorders · LABORATORY INVESTIGATIONS (part 1)

items.statement: Synovial fluid analysis for glucose, protein, lactate dehydrogenase, lactic acid, or autoantibodies is recommended for diagnostic purposes.
items.explanation: These tests are not recommended as they have no diagnostic value.
items.reference: Approach to Articular and Musculoskeletal Disorders · LABORATORY INVESTIGATIONS (part 2)

items.statement: A positive ANA test in isolation is highly specific for lupus.
items.explanation: Positive ANAs are found in many conditions and only a small percentage of positives are due to lupus alone.
items.reference: Approach to Articular and Musculoskeletal Disorders · LABORATORY INVESTIGATIONS (part 2)

items.statement: Scintigraphy is the preferred method for evaluating patients with undiagnosed fever or systemic illness.
items.explanation: FDG-PET/CT scans, not scintigraphy, are useful in evaluating patients with undiagnosed fever or systemic illness.
items.reference: Approach to Articular and Musculoskeletal Disorders · DIAGNOSTIC IMAGING (part 1) | DIAGNOSTIC IMAGING (part 2)

items.statement: MRI is more sensitive than CT for diagnosing soft tissue injuries.
items.explanation: Due to its enhanced soft tissue resolution, MRI is more sensitive than CT in diagnosing soft tissue injuries.
items.reference: Approach to Articular and Musculoskeletal Disorders · DIAGNOSTIC IMAGING (part 1) | DIAGNOSTIC IMAGING (part 2)

items.assertion: Articular disorders often present with deep or diffuse pain.
items.reason: Articular structures include the synovium, synovial fluid, and articular cartilage.
items.explanation: The reason correctly explains why articular disorders present with deep or diffuse pain.
items.reference: Approach to Articular and Musculoskeletal Disorders · INFLAMMATORY OR NONINFLAMMATORY

items.assertion: Noninflammatory conditions are typically associated with elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR).
items.reason: Elevated ESR is a marker of inflammation.
items.explanation: The assertion is false because noninflammatory conditions do not typically have elevated ESR.
items.reference: Approach to Articular and Musculoskeletal Disorders · INFLAMMATORY OR NONINFLAMMATORY

items.assertion: Early consideration of fibromyalgia can prevent unnecessary investigations.
items.reason: Fibromyalgia is often misdiagnosed or not considered, leading to needless investigation and therapy.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains why early consideration of fibromyalgia can prevent unnecessary investigations.
items.reference: Approach to Articular and Musculoskeletal Disorders · FIBROMYALGIA | THE ELDERLY PATIENT

items.assertion: Acute gout may arise during hospitalization and immobilization.
items.reason: Overly aggressive laboratory testing can yield abnormal findings better explained by preexisting conditions.
items.explanation: Both statements are true, but the reason does not explain the assertion.
items.reference: Approach to Articular and Musculoskeletal Disorders · THE HOSPITALIZED PATIENT

items.assertion: Limitation of motion or contractures are frequently caused by inflammation, effusion, pain, deformity, or neuromuscular causes.
items.reason: These factors can restrict joint movement and lead to contractures over time.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion.
items.reference: Approach to Articular and Musculoskeletal Disorders · MUSCULOSKELETAL EXAMINATION

items.assertion: The identification of soft tissue or nonarticular pain is unnecessary if articular complaints are present.
items.reason: Nonarticular involvement can often be overlooked but should be assessed to prevent unwarranted evaluations.
items.explanation: The assertion is false; identifying nonarticular pain is important even when articular complaints exist.
items.reference: Approach to Articular and Musculoskeletal Disorders · MUSCULOSKELETAL EXAMINATION

items.assertion: Bilateral hand pain suggests a degenerative or systemic etiology.
items.reason: Unilateral hand pain is more commonly associated with trauma, overuse, infection, or crystal-induced arthritis.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion.
items.reference: Approach to Articular and Musculoskeletal Disorders · REGIONAL RHEUMATIC COMPLAINTS | HAND PAIN

items.assertion: Dorsal or ventral deformities in hand joints are typical of osteoarthritis.
items.reason: Swan neck or boutonnière deformities are characteristic of rheumatoid arthritis.
items.explanation: The assertion is false, while the reason correctly describes RA-related deformities.
items.reference: Approach to Articular and Musculoskeletal Disorders · REGIONAL RHEUMATIC COMPLAINTS | HAND PAIN

items.assertion: Rotator cuff pathology is often confirmed by MRI or ultrasound.
items.reason: MRI and ultrasound are imaging modalities that provide detailed views of soft tissues, including tendons.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains why MRI or ultrasound confirm rotator cuff pathology.
items.reference: Approach to Articular and Musculoskeletal Disorders · SHOULDER PAIN

items.assertion: Pain with specific active motion suggests a periarticular process.
items.reason: Periarticular processes involve structures around the joint, not within it.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains why pain with specific active motion suggests a periarticular process.
items.reference: Approach to Articular and Musculoskeletal Disorders · SHOULDER PAIN

items.assertion: A positive McMurray test indicates a meniscal tear.
items.reason: The McMurray test involves flexing the knee and rotating it to elicit a click or pain, suggesting a tear.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion.
items.reference: Approach to Articular and Musculoskeletal Disorders · KNEE PAIN

items.assertion: Genu varum results from asymmetric cartilage loss laterally.
items.reason: Asymmetric cartilage loss leads to malalignment in the knee.
items.explanation: The assertion is false; genu varum results from medial cartilage loss, not lateral.
items.reference: Approach to Articular and Musculoskeletal Disorders · KNEE PAIN

items.assertion: Telemedicine is appropriate for routine disease assessments.
items.reason: Telemedicine allows for virtual patient evaluations with a time, cost, and convenience advantage.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason explains why telemedicine is suitable for routine assessments.
items.reference: Approach to Articular and Musculoskeletal Disorders · HIP PAIN | TELEHEALTH EVALUATION

items.assertion: Laboratory tests should be used to confirm a specific clinical diagnosis.
items.reason: They are not useful for screening or evaluating patients with vague rheumatic complaints.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains why laboratory tests should be used.
items.reference: Approach to Articular and Musculoskeletal Disorders · LABORATORY INVESTIGATIONS (part 1)

items.assertion: The presence of high titers of anti-CCP or RF antibodies, or double positivity, may indicate a greater risk for more severe, erosive polyarthritis in RA.
items.reason: Both serum RF and anti-CCP antibodies are comparably sensitive but CCP antibodies are more specific than RF.
items.explanation: While both statements are true, the reason does not explain why high titers or double positivity indicate a greater risk.
items.reference: Approach to Articular and Musculoskeletal Disorders · LABORATORY INVESTIGATIONS (part 2)

items.assertion: Septic arthritis typically presents with synovial fluid that has low viscosity and a WBC count usually >50,000/μL.
items.reason: Such effusions are typical of septic arthritis but may also occur with RA or gout.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion.
items.reference: Approach to Articular and Musculoskeletal Disorders · LABORATORY INVESTIGATIONS (part 2)

items.assertion: Ultrasonography is useful for detecting synovial cysts and rotator cuff tears.
items.reason: Lower cost, better technology, and enhanced site-specific transducers allow for wider use and diagnostic specificity of ultrasonography.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains why ultrasonography is useful in these cases.
items.reference: Approach to Articular and Musculoskeletal Disorders · DIAGNOSTIC IMAGING (part 1) | DIAGNOSTIC IMAGING (part 2)

items.assertion: MRI has replaced scintigraphy for all musculoskeletal imaging applications.
items.reason: MRI provides multiplanar images with fine anatomic detail and contrast resolution.
items.explanation: While MRI is preferred in many cases, it has not completely replaced scintigraphy for all applications; the reason provided does not justify complete replacement.
items.reference: Approach to Articular and Musculoskeletal Disorders · DIAGNOSTIC IMAGING (part 1) | DIAGNOSTIC IMAGING (part 2)
